does: This paper proposes Alignment with Integrated Human Feedback (AIHF), a unified single-stage approach that jointly learns reward models and policies from both demonstration data and preference feedback simultaneously. Rather than decomposing alignment into sequential stages of supervised fine-tuning, reward modeling, and reinforcement learning, AIHF integrates these components into one cohesive training process. The method encompasses a suite of efficient algorithms that generalize existing approaches like RLHF and DPO, requiring only minor modifications to current alignment pipelines. The authors demonstrate that this joint training reduces data under-utilization and eliminates distribution mismatch between the learned reward model and the resulting policy, ultimately achieving improved alignment performance with human values in foundation models and embodied AI systems.
connects: This work directly addresses limitations of the popular RLHF pipeline that has dominated recent alignment research following the success of InstructGPT and related models. RLHF's sequential decomposition into SFT, reward modeling, and RL stages has become the standard approach for aligning large language models with human preferences, but creates inefficiencies through data redundancy and domain shift problems. The paper builds on concurrent work in preference-based learning and direct policy optimization methods like DPO that have questioned the necessity of explicit reward modeling. By incorporating both demonstrations and preferences—drawing from curriculum learning and inverse reinforcement learning traditions—AIHF synthesizes insights from imitation learning and preference learning communities, responding to growing evidence that unified objectives can be more sample-efficient than multi-stage approaches.
enables: This work opens possibilities for more data-efficient and computationally streamlined alignment procedures that could scale to increasingly large models and more complex tasks. The unified framework potentially enables better transfer of demonstration knowledge into preference-aligned policies, which could be particularly valuable for embodied AI and robotics where demonstrations and preference feedback may come from different sources or modalities. The generality of AIHF across multiple algorithm variants suggests it could serve as a foundation for future alignment methods incorporating additional signal types—such as active learning, critiques, or multi-agent preferences. Furthermore, by reducing distribution mismatch and improving sample efficiency, this approach may make alignment more feasible for resource-constrained settings and could facilitate better understanding of how different feedback modalities interact during policy learning.
